steps.title: Determine the underlined portion's role in the argument
steps.content:
The underlined portion states that participants' eye movements in dreams closely matched how sighted awake people track real objects. This observation is then used to justify the team's conclusion that dreams are perceived as more than pure imagination. The underlined portion does not state a consequence, shift the study's focus, or undermine the hypothesis — it provides a comparable circumstance (awake tracking) as a benchmark that gives meaning to the dream-tracking data.
  • Choice A — To illustrate an important real-world implication of Ellison and team's main finding.: An implication would be something that follows from the finding — but the underlined portion is evidence leading to the conclusion, not a consequence of it. Violates the direction of the logic. ✗
  • Choice B — To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify Ellison and team's conclusion.: The underlined portion introduces the comparable case of awake visual tracking, which serves as the standard that justifies inferring the dream objects are perceived as real. Matches the function precisely. ✓
  • Choice C — To show the unexpected result that led Ellison and team to change the focus of their study.: Nothing in the passage suggests the team changed focus or that this result was unexpected — this misreads the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — To offer key evidence that undermines Ellison and team's initial hypothesis.: The underlined portion supports the team's conclusion, not undermines it. Contradicts the passage. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: The underlined portion establishes a parallel with awake visual tracking, providing the comparative baseline that justifies the team's conclusion about dream perception. That matches Choice B — To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify Ellison and team's conclusion..
